notes: BEL clicks the speaker and does not alter the glyph plane or cursor.
BS moves the cursor one column left without erasing.
BS permits overwriting the previous cell and does not erase by itself.
BS at the left edge clamps at column 1.
HT advances from column 1 to the next 8-column tab stop at column 9.
HT from an arbitrary column advances to the next 8-column tab stop.
HT near the right edge clamps at column 80.
CR returns to column 1 on the same row.
LF moves down one row and preserves the column.
LF at the bottom margin scrolls content up and leaves a blank bottom line.
VT acts as LF: the cursor moves down one row with the column preserved (LNM off, so no CR).
FF acts as LF: the cursor moves down one row with the column preserved (LNM off, so no CR).

ESC)0 designates G1 as DEC special graphics; SO (LS1) invokes G1 as the active set, so q/x render as the IIe line-draw fold -/|. If SO were ignored, G0 (ASCII) would stay active and the row would read 'qx', so this case fails unless the locking shift takes effect. basis=profile because the asserted glyphs are the documented Apple IIe ASCII fold of DEC line drawing.

ESC)0 designates G1 as DEC special graphics; SO invokes G1 (q folds to '-'), then SI (LS0) returns GL to G0 so the next q renders as ASCII 'q'. If SI were ignored, G1 would stay active and the row would read '--'; if SO were also ignored it would read 'qq'. The expected '-q' therefore fails unless SO and SI both switch the active set. basis=profile because the '-' is the IIe fold of DEC line drawing.

spec_ref: ECMA-48 §5.4 C0 in control sequences; §8.3 SO (LS1)
notes: ESC)0 designates G1 as DEC special graphics. The SO (0x0E) is embedded mid-CSI, inside 'ESC [ 2 <SO> J': per ECMA-48 the C0 control must execute (invoke G1) without aborting the control sequence, so the pending ED (J) still completes with parameter 2 (erase all) and the trailing q then renders through the now-active G1 as the folded '-'. If SO aborted the CSI, 'J' would print literally; if SO were merely consumed as a CSI intermediate (not executed), G0 would stay active and q would render as ASCII 'q'. Either failure mode yields something other than '-', so this case is discriminating for both state preservation and global execution.

spec_ref: ECMA-48 §5.4 C0 in escape sequences; §8.3 SO (LS1)
notes: The SO (0x0E) is embedded inside the G1 charset-designation sequence, between 'ESC )' and its final designator '0'. SO must execute (invoke G1) without aborting the designation, so 'ESC ) <SO> 0' still designates G1 as DEC special graphics and the following q renders through the active G1 as '-'. If SO aborted or was consumed as the designator byte, G1 would stay ASCII, the literal '0' would print, and q would render as ASCII 'q' -> '0q', so the expected '-' fails unless SO executes while preserving the charset-designation state.
